title: Transliteration & Encoding Quiz
description: A cross-dictionary skill quiz on the CDSL transliteration schemes (SLP1, Devanāgarī, IAST/roman, Harvard-Kyoto, ITRANS), why SLP1 is the internal encoding, Vedic-accent marking, and the transcoder. Applies to every dictionary on the site, not one dictionary's structure.

source.note: Every answer is grounded in the Encoding & Transliteration page (itself verified against the live displays, the API, and the sanskrit-transcoding tables). Conversions use the rāma / śiva worked examples from that page's scheme table.

schemeKey: Display/API scheme values: slp1 (internal storage), deva (Devanāgarī), roman (IAST diacritics), hk (Harvard-Kyoto), itrans (ITRANS).

questions.question: Which transliteration scheme does CDSL use internally to store all of its Sanskrit text?
questions.answer: SLP1 (Sanskrit Library Phonetic Basic) — one ASCII character per phoneme.

questions.question: The word rāma is written identically in which three ASCII schemes (and what is that spelling)?
questions.answer: SLP1, Harvard-Kyoto, and ITRANS — all write it rAma. None of rāma's sounds diverge across those ASCII schemes, so for this word the input scheme barely matters.

questions.question: Which classes of sound actually diverge across the ASCII schemes (so that choosing the right input scheme matters)?
questions.answer: The sibilants (ś, ṣ), the vocalic ṛ / ḷ, and the retroflex consonants.

questions.question: In Harvard-Kyoto, the sound ś (as in śiva) is written:
questions.explanation: SLP1 writes it S, ITRANS writes it sh, Harvard-Kyoto writes it z — three ASCII spellings of one letter.

questions.question: In ITRANS, the sound ś (as in śiva) is written:
Which scheme is described as “Roman with diacritics (IAST-style)”?
Two transcoders exist but are NOT offered in the display input/output menus. Name them and what each is.
questions.answer: wx (used at Hyderabad University) and as (Anglicized Sanskrit, Thomas Malten's letter-number scheme).

questions.question: Why does CDSL store everything in SLP1 internally?
questions.answer: SLP1 is lossless and unambiguous — every Sanskrit phoneme maps to exactly one ASCII character, so sorting, searching, and round-tripping are deterministic.

questions.question: In what encoding is the downloadable XML for each dictionary?
Write śiva (IAST) in SLP1.
Write śiva (IAST) in Harvard-Kyoto.
Write śiva (IAST) in ITRANS.
Write śiva in Devanāgarī.
Write rāma (IAST) in SLP1.
Write rāma in Devanāgarī.
The input box is set to slp1. You want to look up śiva — what exactly do you type?
questions.note: the capital S is SLP1's ś; lowercase s would be the dental s.

questions.question: The input box is set to hk (Harvard-Kyoto). You want śiva — what do you type?
How is the udātta (high pitch) accent written in the SLP1 source?
questions.answer: As / attached to its vowel — e.g. Monier-Williams keys agni as agni/ (accent on the final -i, agní).

questions.question: Do you have to type the Vedic accent to make a word match?
questions.answer: No. Lookup uses the plain search key <k1> (e.g. agni); the accent lives on a separate display key <k2> (agni/). The accent is for display, not matching — typing agni finds the word either way.

questions.question: Which of these dictionaries marks NO Vedic accents?
questions.explanation: MW, PWG/PW, and Grassmann all mark accents; Grassmann centrally, as a Ṛg-Veda dictionary. Apte does not.

questions.question: What does the accent=yes|no toggle (and API parameter) govern?
questions.answer: Whether accents appear in the Devanāgarī output.

questions.question: How do anudātta and svarita appear in the entry body of the German and Grassmann sources, and how does an accent render in Roman?
questions.answer: anudātta and svarita are written \ and ^ in the source body; in Roman an accent renders as a combining acute (á, ā́).

questions.question: You have a diacritic Roman word (rāma). Which input mode should you select?
You only have an ASCII keyboard (no diacritics). Which input schemes let you avoid diacritics entirely?
Your lookup failed. What is the single most likely cause?
questions.answer: An input-scheme mismatch (e.g. typing roman while the box expects hk). Re-check the selected input scheme first.

questions.question: Who originally developed the project's transcoder, and in what language (with later ports)?
questions.answer: Ralph Bunker, in Java — later ported to PHP (transcoder.php) and Python (funderburkjim/sanskrit-transcoding).

questions.question: How does the transcoder define each scheme-to-scheme conversion?
questions.answer: From per-pair XML tables named {X}_{Y}.xml (e.g. slp1_deva.xml), compiled into a finite-state machine at runtime.

questions.question: What does transcoder_processString('rAma', 'slp1', 'deva') return?
